title.emphasis: Phase 2: The Transformation to a "Use" Instrument (Class A Membership)

section.para: The truly innovative part of your model is what happens upon the successful construction of the housing unit. At the moment the Class B membership converts to a Class A membership and the owner takes occupancy, the "economic reality" of the instrument fundamentally changes.
This is where the analysis shifts from California's risk capital test to the federal standard established in United Housing Foundation, Inc. v. Forman.5 The Supreme Court in

Forman held that when the primary motivation for purchasing a share in a cooperative is for personal use and consumption—in that case, to acquire an apartment to live in—it is not a securities transaction.5

listitem.para: Shift from Investment to Consumption: Once the building exists, the member is no longer a passive investor risking capital on a future enterprise. They are now a resident using the asset for its intended purpose: housing.1
Elimination of Risk: The primary "risk" has been eliminated. The member's capital is no longer funding a speculative development; it is now tied to a tangible, existing asset that they occupy.
Transformation of the Asset: The instrument transforms from a security (a claim on a future benefit dependent on the efforts of others) into a non-security (a right of use and occupancy).

section.para: Your proposed structure creates a dynamic life cycle for the cooperative share. It begins its life as a publicly-tradable "risk capital" security (Class B) to finance development, and upon success, it matures into a private, non-tradable "use" instrument (Class A) for the resident-member. This is a powerful model that leverages the specific nuances of securities law to facilitate the financing and creation of new cooperative housing.
